title: LIHTC: The Good, the Bad, and the Very Complicated
why: The whole series. If you read one thing about LIHTC, read this. Start with 'The Only Tool in the Box' and Alan Mallach's 'How to Really Reform the LIHTC Program.'

title: LIHTC for Regular People
why: The plain-English explainer. Written for tenants, not developers. Includes how to look up your own building.

title: What Can Be Done When LIHTC Affordability Restrictions Expire?
why: Seniors in Clovis, California told their rent would double. The clearest account of what expiration does to real people — and how badly the data is tracked.

title: States Are Using This Tool to Keep LIHTC Housing Affordable for Longer
why: The Vermont story. Permanent affordability since the early 2000s, and developer demand still runs two to three times the available credits. This is the article that kills the 'it'll chill investment' argument.

title: LIHTC Lawsuit in Missouri Says Owners Broke Opt-Out Rules
why: Tenants suing owners who used the qualified contract loophole and allegedly didn't tell them for two years. The 15-year exit, in practice.

title: LIHTC Affordability Expirations and the Supply of Guaranteed Affordable Housing
why: Where the 845,000 figure comes from — units scheduled to lose affordability 2024–2035. A third of everything currently restricted. Cite this one to policymakers.

title: Study Examines What Happens to LIHTC Units When Restrictions Expire
why: NLIHC's read on the Freddie Mac study. Note the caveats: for-profit owners exit more; the reassuring national averages don't hold for deeply-targeted units in hot markets.

title: We Have the Least Housing Where Renters Need It Most
why: 703,628 households need rents below $1,100. There were 2,297 such apartments available. The single best rebuttal to 'just build more.'

title: Testimony: New York City's Housing Emergency
why: Fewer than 5,000 apartments available to the 1.17 million households earning under $50,000. Over 33,000 available to those earning above $200,000. Same city.

title: Tenant Opportunity to Purchase Act (TOPA) — S10169
why: The bill text. Reintroduced May 2026 by Sen. Salazar, after Myrie carried it in prior sessions. Community Service Society polling puts New Yorker support at 82%.

title: Community Opportunity to Purchase Act (COPA)
why: The scoreboard. Passed the Council in December 2025, vetoed by Adams, override failed in January, reintroduced in May by CM Sandy Nurse with Mamdani co-signing.

title: A659 — Requiring landlords to disclose the expiration date
why: Passed both houses in 2026. Requires landlords to state the exact date a 421-a benefit expires, in 12-point type, with a $1,000 fine for lying. It does not cover LIHTC-only buildings. That's the gap we're trying to close.

title: Brooklyn Tenants Brace for 'Doomsday' As Their Affordable Housing Deal Nears Its End
why: 63 Tiffany Place. Rent of $1,200 against market listings of $3,214 to $6,300. This is what a LIHTC expiration looks like from inside the building.
